narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 63.9% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your position in Year 3 where you ranked in the bottom decile of your school—far below the middle-to-upper deciles typical for those who passed the bar—making it the single biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Your Year 1 decile ranking in the second lowest group also contributed significantly in the same direction, as it signaled early struggles compared to the top-half averages among successful bar takers. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, while below the 3.24 average for passers, actually nudged the prediction slightly toward success, softening the overall risk assessment a bit. These three elements—your Year 3 and Year 1 deciles as the top concerns, tempered by your undergrad performance—dominated the model's thinking for your profile. Your LSAT score of 27, well under the 37 average for those who passed, played a supporting role in the concern, as did being in the upper-middle family income bracket, which aligns with about 45% of successful students but didn't outweigh the academic flags. Enrolling full-time, like 93% of bar passers, was a neutral to positive signal in your favor. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with stronger early and later undergrad deciles plus higher test scores and GPAs, your metrics placed you in a higher-risk group for bar success, which is why the model leaned against admission. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience and full-time commitment. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools like this one. You're clearly driven, and with targeted improvements, the data will reflect that. Keep pushing—you've got this.
